bytes.topic: LLM Fundamentals — tokens, context window, temperature, top-p, sampling strategies
bytes.title: Groq Sampling Demystified — Temperature, Top-p, and Context Windows in Practice
bytes.body:
Groq's LPU processes tokens at 500–800 tokens/sec, making sampling parameter choices immediately observable. Every string Groq receives is tokenized by the model's native tokenizer — llama-3.3-70b-versatile averages ~3–4 chars per token.

Context window is a hard ceiling: llama-3.3-70b-versatile supports 128k tokens; mixtral-8x7b-32768 caps at 32k. Groq enforces this strictly — exceeding it throws a 400 with context_length_exceeded.

Temperature controls entropy in the output distribution. Set 0 for deterministic tasks (JSON extraction, code gen, classification). Use 0.7–1.0 for open-ended generation. Groq's API accepts values from 0 to 2.

Top-p (nucleus sampling) truncates the probability mass below a cumulative threshold. Groq's own docs follow OpenAI's guidance: set top_p=1.0 when tuning temperature, and temperature=1.0 when tuning top_p — compounding both amplifies randomness unpredictably.

max_tokens hard-caps output. Groq bills on prompt + completion tokens combined. Use response.usage.prompt_tokens and response.usage.completion_tokens to instrument your pipelines. At 700 T/s, a 1k-token completion completes in ~1.4 seconds wall time.
bytes.code_snippet:
from groq import Groq

client = Groq()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",  # 128k context window
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    temperature=0.2,   # deterministic — ideal for structured extraction
    top_p=1.0,         # keep at 1.0 when tuning temperature
    max_tokens=512,    # hard cap; controls cost + latency
)

usage = response.usage
print(f"Prompt: {usage.prompt_tokens} | Completion: {usage.completion_tokens}")
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

bytes.topic: Prompt Engineering — system prompts, few-shot examples, chain of thought, structured output
bytes.title: Groq Prompt Engineering — System Prompts, Few-Shot, CoT, and Structured Output
bytes.body:
Groq's LPU inference speed makes iterative prompt engineering practical — dozens of variations in seconds. These four techniques compose well on llama-3.3-70b-versatile.

System prompts act as persistent context. Keep them under 200 tokens — bloated system prompts inflate time-to-first-token even on LPU hardware. Be directive: 'You are a JSON API. Never explain. Only output valid JSON.'

Few-shot examples belong as interleaved user/assistant pairs in the message history, not stuffed into the system prompt. This preserves the conversation structure the model was fine-tuned on and avoids role confusion.

Chain of thought is triggered with "Think step by step before responding" in the final user message. On deepseek-r1-distill-llama-70b, CoT is built-in — the <think> block streams before the final answer, giving you reasoning traces for free.

Structured output uses response_format={"type": "json_object"} in the completions call. Groq does not enforce JSON Schema (unlike OpenAI's strict mode), so anchor the shape with a few-shot example and drop temperature to 0.2 for determinism.
bytes.code_snippet:
from groq import Groq

client = Groq()

completion = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
    temperature=0.2,
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a JSON API. Classify bugs. Never explain."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Classify: 'segfault in malloc'"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": '{"category": "memory", "severity": "high", "lang": "c"}'},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Think step by step, then classify: 'NullPointerException in Spring Boot controller'"}
    ],
)

print(completion.choices[0].message.content)

bytes.topic: Hugging Face — model hub, transformers library, pipelines, tokenizers
bytes.title: Groq + Hugging Face Tokenizers — Estimate Tokens Before You Pay for Them
bytes.body:
Groq's inference API bills and rate-limits by token count, but the API only tells you after the call completes. You can front-run this by loading the same Hugging Face tokenizer Groq uses server-side and counting locally — zero latency, zero API cost.

For `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`, the underlying tokenizer is `meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B` (same vocab). Load it once with `AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained`, call `.encode()` on your assembled prompt string, and check `len(tokens)` against Groq's 128k context cap before you ever hit the network.

This pattern is especially useful when you're dynamically assembling RAG context: retrieve chunks, tokenize the candidate prompt, trim until it fits, then fire the Groq call. No truncation surprises, no `context_length_exceeded` errors mid-stream. The tokenizer runs in-process in ~1ms, so it adds no meaningful latency to your pipeline. Pair it with `tiktoken` awareness: Groq's Llama models do NOT use the OpenAI BPE vocab — mismatching tokenizers gives counts off by 10–20%.
bytes.code_snippet:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
from groq import Groq

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B")
client = Groq()

def safe_chat(system: str, user: str, model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", max_ctx=120_000):
    prompt = f"{system}\n{user}"
    token_count = len(tokenizer.encode(prompt))
    if token_count > max_ctx:
        raise ValueError(f"Prompt too long: {token_count} tokens (limit {max_ctx})")

    return client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "system", "content": system},
                  {"role": "user",   "content": user}],
    )

bytes.topic: LangChain — chains, agents, tools, memory, LCEL
bytes.title: Groq + LangChain LCEL — Building Fast Chains with ChatGroq
bytes.body:
Groq's inference speed makes streaming chains genuinely practical — latency is low enough that LCEL pipelines feel synchronous. Swap `ChatOpenAI` for `ChatGroq` and you get the full LangChain expression language with Llama 3.3 70B or Mixtral underneath.

The `ChatGroq` class from `langchain_groq` accepts `model_name`, `temperature`, and `streaming`. Pipe it into any `PromptTemplate` using `|` and call `.stream()` to get token-by-token output at Groq's 500+ tok/s throughput. Memory works identically — wrap the chain in `RunnableWithMessageHistory` and pass a `get_session_history` callable.

For tool-calling agents, use `create_tool_calling_agent` with `ChatGroq(model_name='llama-3.3-70b-versatile')` — Groq supports native function calling on this model. Bind tools via `.bind_tools([...])` before the agent node. Because Groq responses are so fast, multi-hop ReAct loops that would feel sluggish on other providers complete in under two seconds, making agentic workflows viable in user-facing products without streaming hacks.
bytes.code_snippet:
from langchain_groq import ChatGroq
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

llm = ChatGroq(model_name="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", temperature=0, streaming=True)

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a concise technical assistant."),
    ("human", "{question}")
])

chain = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()

for chunk in chain.stream({"question": "What is LCEL?"}):
    print(chunk, end="", flush=True)

bytes.topic: LlamaIndex — document loading, indexing strategies, query engines, retrieval
bytes.title: Groq + LlamaIndex — Fast RAG Pipelines with Sub-100ms LLM Calls
bytes.body:
LlamaIndex's Groq integration ships as `llama-index-llms-groq`. Wire it through the global `Settings` object — this propagates Groq as the LLM to every query engine, agent, and retriever built in the session. Groq's sub-100ms token generation fundamentally changes RAG design: multi-step engines like `SubQuestionQueryEngine` and `RouterQueryEngine` become practical because each LLM hop is nearly instant instead of a 2–4s bottleneck.

For indexing, `VectorStoreIndex.from_documents()` chunks docs and builds an in-memory HNSW index. Note: Groq provides no embeddings endpoint, so pair it with a local model like `BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5` via `HuggingFaceEmbedding`. Tune `similarity_top_k` to 3–5 for Llama 3.3 70B's 128k context — higher values bloat the prompt without improving synthesis. For production, swap the default in-memory store for `PGVectorStore` or `ChromaVectorStore`; the `Settings.llm` binding is unchanged. Use `response_mode="tree_summarize"` on large document sets — it recursively summarizes retrieved nodes and Groq's speed makes the extra LLM calls nearly free.
bytes.code_snippet:
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader, Settings
from llama_index.llms.groq import Groq
from llama_index.embeddings.huggingface import HuggingFaceEmbedding

Settings.llm = Groq(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", api_key="gsk_...")
Settings.embed_model = HuggingFaceEmbedding(model_name="BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5")

docs = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(docs)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    similarity_top_k=4,
    response_mode="tree_summarize"
)
response = query_engine.query("What are the key API rate limits?")
print(response)

bytes.topic: RAG — chunking strategy, embedding, retrieval, reranking
bytes.title: Groq RAG — LPU-Powered Reranking with llama-3.1-8b-instant
bytes.body:
Groq's LPU makes LLM-based reranking practical — what's prohibitively slow on GPU inference becomes sub-200ms here. A production Groq RAG pipeline has four stages.

**Chunking:** Recursive splitting at ~512 tokens with 10% overlap (50 tokens). Smaller chunks improve retrieval precision; overlap preserves cross-boundary context. Tag each chunk with source metadata for citations.

**Embedding:** Use a dense retrieval model (e.g., `nomic-embed-text-v1.5`) and store vectors in pgvector with an HNSW index (`ef_construction=128`, `m=16`) for sub-millisecond ANN queries.

**Retrieval:** Fetch top-20 candidates via cosine similarity — cast wide here, reranking handles precision.

**Reranking with Groq:** Use `llama-3.1-8b-instant` to score each candidate chunk 1–10 for relevance. At Groq's throughput, you can fire all 20 scoring requests concurrently, collapsing reranking to a single async round-trip. Pass only the top-3 chunks to `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` for final generation. This two-model pattern cuts hallucinations without meaningful latency overhead — the fast model filters noise, the capable model synthesizes signal.
bytes.code_snippet:
import asyncio
from groq import AsyncGroq

client = AsyncGroq()

async def score_chunk(query: str, chunk: str) -> float:
    resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="llama-3.1-8b-instant",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Score relevance 1-10.\nQuery: {query}\nPassage: {chunk}\nRespond with only a number."}],
        max_tokens=3,
    )
    return float(resp.choices[0].message.content.strip())

async def rerank(query: str, chunks: list[str], top_k: int = 3) -> list[str]:
    scores = await asyncio.gather(*[score_chunk(query, c) for c in chunks])
    ranked = sorted(zip(scores, chunks), reverse=True)
    return [c for _, c in ranked[:top_k]]

async def rag(query: str, chunks: list[str]) -> str:
    top = await rerank(query, chunks)
    resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Answer using:\n" + "\n\n".join(top)},
            {"role": "user", "content": query},
        ],
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content

bytes.topic: OpenAI API — chat completions, function calling, structured outputs, embeddings
bytes.title: Groq Function Calling — Structured Outputs at LPU Speed
bytes.body: Groq's OpenAI-compatible API means you can drop it in anywhere you'd use OpenAI — but the payoff is latency: Llama 3.3 70B on Groq returns tool calls in ~300ms vs. seconds elsewhere. Use `tool_choice: "required"` to force a tool call and guarantee structured output without a JSON-mode fallback. Define your schema as a JSON Schema function, then parse `response.choices[0].message.tool_calls[0].function.arguments` directly — it's already a JSON string. For strict structured outputs, pair this with Zod or Pydantic validation on your side; Groq doesn't enforce schema adherence server-side like OpenAI's `strict: true` mode. Note: Groq has no native embeddings endpoint — use a separate provider (e.g. `text-embedding-3-small` via OpenAI, or a local model) and store vectors in pgvector. The real Groq advantage is agentic loops: because each tool-call round-trip is so fast, you can run 5–10 LLM hops in the time a single GPT-4o call takes.

bytes.topic: Anthropic API — Claude models, system prompts, tool use, streaming
bytes.title: groq Parallel Tool Use — Fan Out, Collect, and Stream the Synthesis
bytes.body: Groq sets `parallel_tool_calls: true` by default on supported models, meaning the LLM emits multiple function calls in a single inference pass before your code executes any of them. This is the right primitive when a user query requires hitting several APIs simultaneously — no sequential chaining, no extra round trips. Pass your tools array as OpenAI-compatible JSON schemas; Groq validates them server-side. Use `tool_choice: "required"` to guarantee at least one call, or pass `{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "..."}}`to pin to a single tool. After executing your functions, push each result back as `{role: "tool", tool_call_id, content}` — order must match the original `tool_calls` array. For the final synthesis turn, enable `stream: true`; Groq's LPU throughput makes streaming feel instant even on `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`. Avoid streaming the tool-call turn itself — `delta.tool_calls[i].function.arguments` arrives as fragmented JSON you must manually accumulate, which adds fragility. Non-stream for tool collection, stream for the human-readable answer.

bytes.topic: Groq — inference speed, supported models, API compatibility
bytes.title: Groq's Inference Engine — Speed, Models, and OpenAI API Compatibility
bytes.body:
Groq runs on Language Processing Units (LPUs), not GPUs. The architectural difference is stark: LPUs execute deterministic, single-tenant compute with no memory bandwidth contention, delivering 500–800 tokens/second on Llama 3.3 70B — roughly 10–25x faster than GPU-based providers at comparable model sizes.

Groq's GroqCloud currently supports: `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`, `llama-3.1-8b-instant`, `mixtral-8x7b-32768`, `gemma2-9b-it`, and `llama-guard-3-8b` for content moderation. The `32768` context models (Mixtral) are ideal for long-context retrieval without chunking overhead.

The API is OpenAI-compatible — swap the base URL and key, nothing else changes. This means any LangChain, LlamaIndex, or raw `openai` SDK integration works out of the box. Rate limits on free tier are aggressive (30 req/min for 70B), so cache aggressively and batch where possible. Use `stream: true` to start rendering before full completion — at Groq speeds, streaming is optional UX polish, not a necessity.
bytes.code_snippet:
from groq import Groq

client = Groq(api_key="gsk_...")

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain RLHF in 3 sentences."}],
    temperature=0.2,
    max_tokens=256,
    stream=False,
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)
print(f"Speed: {response.usage.completion_tokens / response.usage.total_time:.0f} tok/s")

bytes.topic: Ollama — local model serving, model library, privacy benefits
bytes.title: Groq SDK + Ollama — Local Dev Without Switching Clients
bytes.body:
Groq's cloud inference is fast, but every request sends your prompt to Groq's servers. For privacy-sensitive prototyping — PII-heavy prompts, proprietary codebases, client data — Ollama lets you run the same model families locally with zero egress. The key insight: Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST endpoint at `http://localhost:11434/v1`, which means the `groq` Python SDK can point directly at it by overriding `base_url`. You keep the same client interface, the same message schema, the same response shape — just pass `api_key="ollama"` (Ollama ignores it) and reference a locally-pulled model.

Ollama's library ships quantized GGUF variants of Llama 3.2, Mistral, Gemma 2, and others — the same families Groq serves on-cloud. Pull with `ollama pull llama3.2`, prototype locally, then restore `base_url` and your real Groq API key to hit Groq's LPU infrastructure for sub-200ms production latency. Your CI gets deterministic offline tests; your prod path gets Groq's speed. No client code changes between environments.
bytes.code_snippet:
from groq import Groq

# Dev: redirect Groq client to local Ollama instance
client = Groq(
    base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1",
    api_key="ollama",  # Ollama ignores this value
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama3.2",  # ollama pull llama3.2
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain GGUF quantization briefly."}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Production: swap back to Groq endpoint + real key — zero other changes needed

bytes.topic: Embeddings — vector representations, semantic similarity, embedding model comparison
bytes.title: Groq RAG Pipelines — Solving the Embedding Gap
bytes.body: Groq's API is inference-only — there are no native embedding endpoints. Every RAG pipeline built on Groq needs a deliberate embedding strategy. The standard pattern: use sentence-transformers locally (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim) or a remote provider like OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small (1536-dim) for retrieval, then hand off to Groq's Llama 3.3 70B for generation. Latency math matters here: Groq delivers sub-100ms LLM responses, but a remote embedding call adds 50–150ms — easily dominating your pipeline. Local MiniLM inference costs ~5ms on CPU and scores ~63.0 on MTEB, nearly matching text-embedding-3-small's ~62.3. For cosine similarity, normalize vectors before dot product — faster and numerically stable. Store vectors in pgvector with an HNSW index (m=16, ef_construction=64) for sub-10ms ANN retrieval. The optimal Groq RAG stack: sentence-transformers local embed → pgvector HNSW lookup → Groq LLaMA generation. This keeps your total round-trip under 200ms, fully exploiting Groq's inference speed advantage.
bytes.code_snippet:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from groq import Groq
import numpy as np

embedder = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
client = Groq()

def rag_query(question: str, docs: list[str]) -> str:
    q_vec = embedder.encode(question, normalize_embeddings=True)
    doc_vecs = embedder.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)
    scores = doc_vecs @ q_vec
    top_doc = docs[np.argmax(scores)]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": f"Context: {top_doc}"},
            {"role": "user", "content": question},
        ],
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

bytes.topic: Fine-tuning — when to fine-tune vs prompt engineer, LoRA, QLoRA
bytes.title: Groq Fine-Tuning vs Prompt Engineering — When LPU Speed Changes the Calculus
bytes.body:
Groq's LPU delivers ~500 tokens/sec — that changes the fine-tune vs prompt-engineer decision tree. Latency is no longer a reason to compress behavior into fine-tuned weights; you can afford 50-shot examples in-context and still respond faster than GPU-hosted fine-tuned models.

**Stick with prompt engineering when:** your dataset is under 500 examples, task format varies, or you need rapid iteration. On Groq, a rich system prompt with dynamic few-shots costs milliseconds, not seconds.

**Fine-tune when:** you need consistent structured output the model resists (strict JSON schemas, proprietary formats), domain vocabulary is highly specialized, or you're hitting token budget ceilings at scale. Target 1,000+ labeled examples before committing.

**LoRA / QLoRA:** LoRA freezes base weights and trains low-rank adapter matrices (rank 8–64). QLoRA adds 4-bit NF4 quantization so a 70B model fits on a single A100. Use `unsloth` or `trl`'s `SFTTrainer` for fast runs.

Critical caveat: Groq is inference-only. Fine-tuned models must be deployed elsewhere — vLLM, Together AI, or self-hosted. Groq will not serve your LoRA adapter. This makes prompt engineering on `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` your primary lever inside Groq's ecosystem.

bytes.topic: Agents & Tool Use — function calling, ReAct pattern, tool selection, agent loops
bytes.title: Groq Tool Use — Building Fast Agent Loops with Function Calling
bytes.body:
Groq's sub-100ms inference latency fundamentally changes agent loop economics. Each ReAct cycle (Reason → Act → Observe) that would take 2–3s on slower providers completes in ~300ms on Groq, making multi-step agents actually usable in real-time UX.

Groq's function calling follows the OpenAI-compatible schema: pass `tools` as an array of JSON Schema definitions, then check `response.choices[0].message.tool_calls`. If `finish_reason === 'tool_calls'`, execute the tool, append the result as a `role: 'tool'` message, and re-invoke — the agent loop.

Key Groq-specific behavior: `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` is the strongest tool-use model on Groq. Set `tool_choice: 'auto'` to let the model decide, or `{ type: 'function', function: { name } }` to force a specific tool. Avoid `parallel_tool_calls: true` unless your tools are truly independent — Groq supports it, but llama-3.3 can hallucinate argument overlap in parallel calls. Cap agent loops at 5–8 iterations with a hard counter to prevent runaway token burn.

bytes.topic: Streaming — streaming responses, token-by-token output, UX patterns
bytes.title: Groq Streaming — Token-by-Token Output with chat.completions.create
bytes.body: Groq's inference speed (often 500+ tokens/sec) makes streaming feel instantaneous, but you still want it — streaming unblocks your UI immediately rather than waiting for a full response buffer. Pass `stream: true` to `client.chat.completions.create()` and you get back an async iterable of `ChatCompletionChunk` objects. Each chunk carries `choices[0].delta.content`, which is `null` on the final chunk when `finish_reason` is set. For UX, append each delta directly to a `ref` (not state) to avoid React re-render thrashing — flush to state every ~50ms with a `setInterval`. Groq supports streaming across all hosted models including `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` and `mixtral-8x7b-32768`. One gotcha: if you're using `tool_calls`, the delta accumulates across chunks — you must concatenate `delta.tool_calls[0].function.arguments` manually before parsing JSON. Abort mid-stream via `AbortController` passed as `signal` in the fetch options, or by breaking out of the async iterator early, which triggers `stream.controller.abort()` automatically.

bytes.topic: Cost Management — token counting, caching, batching, model selection for cost
bytes.title: Groq Cost Management — Token Counting, Model Routing, and Batching
bytes.body:
Groq bills input and output tokens separately — output tokens cost more, so cap every request with `max_tokens`. Every `ChatCompletion` response exposes `response.usage` with `prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens`, and `total_tokens`; log these per call to catch prompt bloat before it compounds.

The highest-leverage cost lever is model routing. Use `llama-3.1-8b-instant` (131K context, ~$0.05/M input) for classification, extraction, and short summaries. Reserve `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` for multi-step reasoning or RAG synthesis. A simple dispatcher keyed on task type can cut costs 60–80% on mixed workloads.

Groq has no native batch endpoint, so parallelize with the async client: `AsyncGroq` + `asyncio.gather()` lets you fire concurrent requests while respecting rate limits (free tier: 30 RPM on most models; paid: up to 14,400 RPD on 70B). Groq does not currently offer prompt caching, so trim static system prompts aggressively — every token repeated across thousands of calls is a direct cost multiplier. Inject dynamic context via the `user` role instead of bloating the `system` prompt.
bytes.code_snippet:
import asyncio
from groq import AsyncGroq

client = AsyncGroq()

ROUTING = {
    "simple": "llama-3.1-8b-instant",
    "complex": "llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
}

async def chat(prompt: str, task_type: str = "simple") -> str:
    response = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model=ROUTING[task_type],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    u = response.usage
    print(f"[{ROUTING[task_type]}] in={u.prompt_tokens} out={u.completion_tokens} total={u.total_tokens}")
    return response.choices[0].message.content

async def batch(prompts: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    return await asyncio.gather(*[chat(p) for p in prompts])

bytes.topic: Evaluation — LLM-as-judge, RAGAS for RAG evaluation, benchmarks
bytes.title: groq as LLM-as-Judge — Fast RAG Evaluation with RAGAS
bytes.body:
Using Groq as the judge LLM in RAGAS slashes evaluation time dramatically — Groq's LPU delivers 500+ tokens/sec, so a 100-sample eval that takes minutes on hosted GPUs finishes in seconds. Pass `ChatGroq` from `langchain-groq` directly into RAGAS's `evaluate()` call and it replaces the default judge model.

RAGAS's core metrics — `faithfulness`, `answer_relevancy`, `context_precision`, and `context_recall` — all require LLM calls under the hood. With `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`, you get strong judge quality at Groq speed. Set `temperature=0` on the judge to keep scores deterministic across runs.

For LLM-as-judge pipelines outside RAGAS, use Groq's OpenAI-compatible `/chat/completions` endpoint with `response_format={"type": "json_object"}` to extract numeric scores reliably. Prompt the judge to return `{"score": 1-5, "reason": "..."}` — Groq's low latency makes iterating on judge prompts fast and cheap.

Benchmark strategy: use `llama-3.1-8b-instant` on Groq for quick sanity-check passes during development, then promote to `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` for final eval runs where judge accuracy matters.
bytes.code_snippet:
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy
from langchain_groq import ChatGroq
from datasets import Dataset

llm = ChatGroq(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", temperature=0)

dataset = Dataset.from_dict({
    "question": ["What is Groq LPU?"],
    "answer": ["Groq's LPU is a purpose-built chip for fast LLM inference."],
    "contexts": [["Groq's Language Processing Unit (LPU) delivers deterministic, low-latency inference."]],
    "ground_truth": ["Groq LPU is a chip designed for fast language model inference."]
})

result = evaluate(dataset, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy], llm=llm)
print(result)

bytes.topic: Safety & Guardrails — output filtering, prompt injection, hallucination mitigation
bytes.title: Groq Safety Guardrails — Llama Guard, Prompt Injection, and Hallucination Mitigation
bytes.body:
Groq hosts Llama Guard 3 (`llama-guard-3-8b`) as a first-class model — use it as an async moderation layer without adding meaningful latency, since Groq's inference speed keeps the round-trip under 200ms. Call it after generation to classify outputs across 14 hazard categories (S1–S14), then gate your response on the result.

For prompt injection, Groq recommends structuring system prompts with explicit trust delimiters: wrap user input in XML-style tags (`<user_input>`) and instruct the model to treat content inside them as untrusted data only. This survives most direct injection attempts against Llama 3.3 70B.

Hallucination mitigation on Groq leans on `temperature: 0` plus `logprobs: true` — expose token-level confidence and reject answers where top-token probability drops below a threshold (e.g. < 0.6) on factual queries. Combine with a retrieval step: pass only grounded context in the system prompt and explicitly instruct the model not to answer outside it. The `stop` parameter can hard-terminate responses that drift past a known sentinel.

bytes.topic: Context Management — long context handling, chunking, sliding window, summarization
bytes.title: Groq Context Management — Sliding Window and Chunking for 128k Token Models
bytes.body: Groq's fastest models vary wildly in context limits: `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` and `llama-3.1-8b-instant` support 128k tokens, while `mixtral-8x7b-32768` caps at 32k. Exceeding these silently truncates or raises a 400. For chat apps, implement a sliding window over `messages[]` — keep the system prompt, drop oldest user/assistant pairs until you're under budget. Track tokens using Groq's `usage.prompt_tokens` from the response object, not a local estimator. For document Q&A, chunk at ~3,000 tokens with 200-token overlap, embed each chunk, retrieve top-k by similarity, then pass only those chunks to Groq. When conversation history itself grows beyond budget, use a summarization pass: send the oldest N turns to `llama-3.1-8b-instant` (fast, cheap) and replace them with a single summary message. Always set `max_tokens` explicitly — Groq won't auto-cap output, and runaway completions eat your remaining context budget. Monitor `x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens` in response headers to avoid hitting TPM limits mid-session.
bytes.code_snippet:
from groq import Groq

client = Groq()
MAX_CONTEXT = 120_000  # leave headroom under 128k

def sliding_window(messages: list[dict], limit: int = MAX_CONTEXT) -> list[dict]:
    system = [m for m in messages if m["role"] == "system"]
    history = [m for m in messages if m["role"] != "system"]
    # rough token estimate: 1 token ≈ 4 chars
    while sum(len(m["content"]) // 4 for m in system + history) > limit:
        history = history[2:]  # drop oldest user+assistant pair
    return system + history

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
    messages=sliding_window(conversation_history),
    max_tokens=1024,
)
print(response.usage.prompt_tokens)  # verify actual token usage
